question_text: A, B and C share profits in 5:3:2. D is admitted for 1/10th share which he acquires entirely from A. The new profit-sharing ratio is:
explanation:
A's old share = 5/10. A gives 1/10 to D. A's new share = 5/10 − 1/10 = 4/10.
B's share remains = 3/10. C's share remains = 2/10. D's share = 1/10.
New ratio = 4:3:2:1.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: New ratio when only one partner sacrifices
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: 5:3:2:1 totals 11 parts and doesn't account for A's sacrifice. A gave 1/10 to D, so A's share reduces from 5/10 to 4/10.
elimination_hints.misconception: Simply appending D's share without reducing A's.

elimination_hints.hint: 3:3:2:2 implies A gave 2/10 and D got 2/10. But D was admitted for 1/10th share only.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect arithmetic in computing the sacrifice.

elimination_hints.hint: D's share cannot be 0 — he was admitted for 1/10th share. This ratio gives D nothing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring D's share entirely.

question_text: P and Q share profits in 3:2. R is admitted for 1/4th share. P and Q decide to share equally in the new firm. The sacrificing ratio of P and Q is:
explanation:
New ratio of P, Q and R: R gets 1/4. P and Q share remaining 3/4 equally, so each gets 3/8.
P's sacrifice = 3/5 − 3/8 = (24 − 15)/40 = 9/40.
Q's sacrifice = 2/5 − 3/8 = (16 − 15)/40 = 1/40.
Sacrificing ratio = 9:1.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: Sacrificing ratio when new sharing changes
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: 3:2 is the old ratio. Sacrificing ratio = Old Share − New Share for each partner. Since P and Q now share equally, their sacrifices differ.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming sacrificing ratio equals old ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: 1:1 would mean both sacrificed equally. But P's old share (3/5) is much larger than Q's (2/5), and both get 3/8. P sacrifices 9/40 while Q sacrifices only 1/40.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming equal sacrifice when old shares are unequal.

elimination_hints.hint: 5:3 doesn't result from the correct calculation. P sacrifices 9/40 and Q sacrifices 1/40, giving ratio 9:1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in fraction subtraction.

question_text: X and Y share profits in 2:1. Z is admitted for 1/3rd share. Z brings ₹60,000 as goodwill premium. If old partners sacrifice in their old ratio, goodwill credited to X and Y will be:
explanation:
Premium = ₹60,000. Sacrificing ratio = Old ratio = 2:1.
X's share = 60,000 × 2/3 = ₹40,000.
Y's share = 60,000 × 1/3 = ₹20,000.
subtopic: Premium credited in sacrificing ratio
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Equal distribution would apply only if X and Y shared equally. They share 2:1, so X gets twice Y's share.
elimination_hints.misconception: Dividing premium equally ignoring the ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the ratio — giving Y twice X's share. X's ratio is 2, Y's is 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the partner shares in the ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: Both partners sacrificed (in 2:1 ratio), so both should receive compensation, not just X.
elimination_hints.misconception: Giving entire premium to only one partner.

question_text: A and B share profits equally. C is admitted for 1/5th share. Goodwill of the firm is ₹5,00,000. C cannot bring goodwill in cash. If goodwill is adjusted through capital accounts, the journal entry for goodwill adjustment is:
explanation:
C's share of goodwill = 1/5 × 5,00,000 = ₹1,00,000. Old partners sacrifice equally (1:1).
Since C cannot bring cash, the adjustment is made through capital accounts:
Dr. C's Capital A/c ₹1,00,000
Cr. A's Capital A/c ₹50,000
Cr. B's Capital A/c ₹50,000
(Goodwill debited to new partner, credited to old partners in sacrificing ratio 1:1)
subtopic: Goodwill adjusted through capitals when cash not brought
subject: accountancy

options.text: Dr. Goodwill A/c ₹1,00,000; Cr. A's Capital ₹50,000; Cr. B's Capital ₹50,000
Dr. C's Capital A/c ₹1,00,000; Cr. A's Capital ₹50,000; Cr. B's Capital ₹50,000
Dr. C's Capital A/c ₹5,00,000; Cr. A's Capital ₹2,50,000; Cr. B's Capital ₹2,50,000
Dr. A's Capital ₹50,000; Dr. B's Capital ₹50,000; Cr. C's Capital ₹1,00,000

elimination_hints.hint: No Goodwill Account is raised here because C cannot bring cash. The adjustment is directly through capital accounts — debit C's capital, credit old partners'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating a Goodwill Account when the method is capital adjustment.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,00,000 is the TOTAL goodwill. C's share is only 1/5 = ₹1,00,000. Only C's proportionate share is adjusted.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using total goodwill instead of new partner's share.

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the entry — old partners should be CREDITED (they receive compensation), not debited. C should be DEBITED (pays for goodwill).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing debit and credit — confusing who pays and who receives.

question_text: When goodwill is raised at full value and then immediately written off on admission of a new partner, the net effect on old partners' capital accounts is:
explanation:
Step 1: Raise goodwill — Dr. Goodwill A/c, Cr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (OLD ratio).
Step 2: Write off goodwill — Dr. ALL Partners' Capital A/cs (NEW ratio), Cr. Goodwill A/c.
Net effect: Old partners gain in old ratio but lose in new ratio. The net effect equals the gain/loss from the difference between old ratio and new ratio shares, which effectively transfers value from sacrificing partners to the new partner via capital accounts.
subtopic: Net effect of raise and write-off method
subject: accountancy

options.text: No net effect — the two entries cancel each other completely
Old partners' capitals increase by the full goodwill amount
Old partners' capitals increase by the difference between their old ratio share and new ratio share of goodwill
Only the new partner's capital is affected

elimination_hints.hint: The entries don't fully cancel because goodwill is raised in OLD ratio but written off in NEW ratio. The different ratios cause a net effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking raise and write-off in different ratios produces zero net effect.

elimination_hints.hint: The full amount is credited when raised but a portion is also debited when written off. The net increase is only the difference between old and new ratio shares.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the write-off step entirely.

elimination_hints.hint: All partners' capitals are affected — old partners in both steps, new partner only in write-off. The net effect impacts everyone.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the raise-and-write-off method only affects the new partner.

question_text: L and M share profits in 3:1. N is admitted for 1/5th share. Goodwill of the firm is valued at ₹4,00,000. If goodwill is raised and written off: L's capital will show a net increase of:
explanation:
Step 1 — Raise in old ratio (3:1): L credited = 4,00,000 × 3/4 = ₹3,00,000.
Step 2 — Write off in new ratio. New ratio: L and M sacrifice in old ratio 3:1. N gets 1/5.
L's new share = 3/4 × 4/5 = 12/20 = 3/5. M's new share = 1/4 × 4/5 = 4/20 = 1/5. N = 1/5.
New ratio = 3:1:1 (or 12:4:4).
Write off: L debited = 4,00,000 × 3/5 = ₹2,40,000.
Net increase for L = 3,00,000 − 2,40,000 = ₹60,000.
subtopic: Numerical — net effect of raise and write-off
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹3,00,000 is only the credit from raising goodwill. You must also debit L's share when writing off goodwill in the new ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only the raise step and ignoring the write-off.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,00,000 would be N's share of goodwill (1/5 × 4,00,000). L's net increase = credit in old ratio − debit in new ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing new partner's share with L's net gain.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹80,000 comes from an incorrect new ratio calculation. L's new share = 3/5, write-off = 2,40,000. Net = 3,00,000 − 2,40,000 = ₹60,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in computing the new ratio or write-off amount.

question_text: On admission of a new partner, the new partner brings goodwill in kind (as an asset other than cash). The journal entry will be:
explanation: When goodwill is brought in kind (e.g., machinery, stock), the specific asset is debited (not Cash or Goodwill A/c), and old partners' capital accounts are credited in the sacrificing ratio. The asset brought replaces the cash that the new partner would have otherwise contributed as goodwill premium.
subtopic: Goodwill brought as asset other than cash
subject: accountancy

options.text: Dr. Goodwill A/c; Cr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (sacrificing ratio)
Dr. Cash/Bank A/c; Cr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (sacrificing ratio)
Dr. Particular Asset A/c; Cr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (sacrificing ratio)
Dr. Particular Asset A/c; Cr. New Partner's Capital A/c

elimination_hints.hint: Goodwill A/c is raised only when goodwill itself appears in the books. Here, a specific asset is brought — that asset is debited, not Goodwill A/c.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing goodwill as an asset with the actual asset brought in.

elimination_hints.hint: Cash/Bank is debited only when goodwill premium is brought in cash. Here the premium is brought as an asset in kind.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming goodwill is always brought in cash.

elimination_hints.hint: The new partner is PAYING the goodwill, so old partners' capitals are credited (they receive), not the new partner's.
elimination_hints.misconception: Crediting the payer instead of the receiving old partners.

question_text: A and B share profits in 2:3. C is admitted for 1/4th share which he acquires 1/8th from A and 1/8th from B. Goodwill of the firm is ₹2,40,000. C brings his share of goodwill in cash. Amount credited to A and B respectively is:
explanation:
C's share of goodwill = 1/4 × 2,40,000 = ₹60,000.
Sacrificing ratio: A sacrifices 1/8, B sacrifices 1/8. Ratio = 1:1.
A credited = 60,000 × 1/2 = ₹30,000.
B credited = 60,000 × 1/2 = ₹30,000.
Note: Premium is distributed in SACRIFICING ratio (1:1), not old ratio (2:3).
subtopic: Premium when sacrificing ratio differs from old ratio
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹24,000 and ₹36,000 distributes in 2:3 (old ratio). But premium is shared in SACRIFICING ratio (1:1), not old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using old ratio instead of sacrificing ratio for goodwill distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹36,000 and ₹24,000 distributes in 3:2 (reverse of old ratio). Neither old nor sacrificing ratio is 3:2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the old ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: Both A and B sacrificed equally (1/8 each), so both must receive equal compensation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Giving entire premium to only one partner.

question_text:
Study the following journal entry passed on admission of a new partner and answer the question:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           JOURNAL ENTRY                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Goodwill A/c          Dr.    ₹3,00,000                 │
│    To A's Capital A/c              ₹1,80,000            │
│    To B's Capital A/c              ₹1,20,000            │
│                                                         │
│ A's Capital A/c       Dr.    ₹1,50,000                 │
│ B's Capital A/c       Dr.    ₹1,00,000                 │
│ C's Capital A/c       Dr.    ₹50,000                   │
│    To Goodwill A/c                 ₹3,00,000            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

What is the new profit-sharing ratio of A, B and C?
explanation:
From the first entry: Goodwill raised in old ratio. A:B = 1,80,000:1,20,000 = 3:2. Old ratio = 3:2.
From the second entry: Goodwill written off in new ratio. A:B:C = 1,50,000:1,00,000:50,000 = 3:2:1. New ratio = 3:2:1.
The write-off entry tells us the new ratio directly since goodwill is written off in the NEW profit-sharing ratio.
subtopic: Inferring ratio from journal entries
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: C's share is not 0. The write-off entry shows C debited ₹50,000 out of ₹3,00,000, meaning C has 1/6 share in the new ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring C's debit in the write-off entry.

elimination_hints.hint: 1,80:1,20:0 is the old ratio (3:2) from the raise entry. The NEW ratio comes from the write-off entry: 1,50:1,00:50 = 3:2:1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reading the raise entry instead of the write-off entry for new ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: 1,50:1,80:50 mixes amounts from both entries. The write-off amounts (1,50:1,00:50) give the new ratio = 3:2:1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up amounts from the two different journal entries.

question_text:
Study the following Balance Sheet extract and answer the question:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        BALANCE SHEET OF M/s P & Q (Before Admission)          │
├──────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Liabilities              │ Assets                             │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Capitals:                │ Sundry Assets    ₹6,00,000         │
│   P's Capital ₹2,50,000 │ Goodwill         ₹50,000           │
│   Q's Capital ₹1,50,000 │                                     │
│ General Reserve ₹1,00,000│                                    │
│ Creditors      ₹1,50,000│                                     │
├──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Total          ₹6,50,000│ Total            ₹6,50,000         │
└──────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┘

P and Q share profits in 3:2. R is admitted for 1/4th share. Goodwill of the firm is valued at ₹2,00,000. The existing goodwill appearing in the Balance Sheet should be:
explanation:
When goodwill already appears in the books and it needs to be revalued, the existing goodwill (₹50,000) must first be written off in the OLD ratio before any new goodwill treatment.
Write off existing goodwill:
Dr. P's Capital A/c ₹30,000 (50,000 × 3/5)
Dr. Q's Capital A/c ₹20,000 (50,000 × 2/5)
Cr. Goodwill A/c ₹50,000
This removes the old goodwill. Then the new goodwill (₹2,00,000) is treated as per the chosen method.
subtopic: Treatment of existing goodwill in books
subject: accountancy

options.text: Written off by debiting old partners' capital accounts in old ratio (3:2)
Written off by debiting all partners' capital accounts in new ratio
Retained in the books at ₹50,000 without any adjustment
Added to the new goodwill valuation making total goodwill ₹2,50,000

elimination_hints.hint: Existing goodwill was created by old partners. R (new partner) had no role in it. It must be written off in OLD ratio, not new ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the new partner in writing off old goodwill.

elimination_hints.hint: Existing goodwill at book value may not reflect true value. As per accounting standards, it must be written off before fresh goodwill treatment on admission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking old goodwill can remain as is during admission.

elimination_hints.hint: Existing goodwill and new goodwill valuation are treated separately. Old goodwill is written off first; new goodwill of ₹2,00,000 is then treated independently.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding old book goodwill to new valuation.

question_text:
Assertion (A): When old partners sacrifice in a ratio different from the old profit-sharing ratio, the goodwill premium brought by the new partner is distributed in the sacrificing ratio, not the old ratio.
Reason (R): The sacrificing ratio represents the proportion in which old partners have given up their share of profits for the new partner.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. The sacrificing ratio measures the actual sacrifice made by each old partner. Since partners who sacrifice more deserve proportionately more compensation, the goodwill premium is distributed in the sacrificing ratio. When sacrifice differs from the old ratio, using the old ratio would unfairly compensate partners.
subtopic: Rationale for using sacrificing ratio
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY premium goes in sacrificing ratio — because it measures the proportion of sacrifice. This is exactly why A is true.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to see the direct causal link between R and A.

elimination_hints.hint: R is absolutely true — sacrificing ratio DOES represent the proportion of profit share given up by each old partner. This is the definition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the definition of sacrificing ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: A is true — when sacrifice differs from old ratio, premium MUST go in sacrificing ratio for fair compensation. This is a fundamental accounting principle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking premium always goes in old ratio regardless.

question_text:
Assertion (A): When the new partner's share is given but the sacrifice by individual old partners is not mentioned, it is assumed that old partners sacrifice in their old ratio.
Reason (R): In the absence of any agreement to the contrary, old partners are presumed to continue sharing profits and bearing sacrifices in their existing ratio.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Section 11(a) of the Indian Partnership Act, 1932 states that in the absence of any agreement, partners share profits equally. By extension, when no specific sacrifice is mentioned, partners sacrifice in their old ratio. R provides the general principle behind this assumption.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: Default sacrifice assumption
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R explains exactly WHY old ratio is assumed — because without contrary agreement, partners continue in their existing ratio. R is the reason behind A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the logical connection between the general principle (R) and the specific rule (A).

elimination_hints.hint: R is true — it states a well-established principle of partnership law that partners share in their agreed ratio unless otherwise decided.
elimination_hints.misconception: Rejecting a correct legal principle.

elimination_hints.hint: A is true — this is a standard accounting rule taught across all textbooks. When individual sacrifice is not specified, old ratio is used.
elimination_hints.misconception: Rejecting a universally accepted accounting convention.

question_text:
State whether the following statement is True or False:

'If the new partner acquires his share from only one of the old partners, then only that old partner's share of profit changes while the other old partner's share remains the same.'
explanation: TRUE. If the new partner acquires his entire share from only one old partner, only that partner sacrifices. The other old partner's share remains unchanged. For example, if A and B share 3:2 and C gets 1/5th entirely from A, then A's new share = 3/5 − 1/5 = 2/5, B remains 2/5, C = 1/5. Only A's share changed.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: Single partner sacrifice
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Consider: if C acquires share entirely from A, then B gives up nothing. B's old share = B's new share. Only A's share reduces by C's share.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking all old partners' shares must change on admission.

question_text:
State whether the following statement is True or False:

'On admission of a new partner, the goodwill premium brought in cash by the new partner is always credited to the old partners in their old profit-sharing ratio.'
explanation: FALSE. The goodwill premium is credited to old partners in the SACRIFICING ratio, not always the old ratio. Sacrificing ratio may or may not equal the old ratio depending on how the new partner acquires the share. Only when old partners sacrifice in their old ratio does sacrificing ratio = old ratio. If sacrifice is specified differently, the distribution follows the sacrificing ratio.
subtopic: Premium distribution ratio
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: The statement says 'always' in old ratio. But when sacrifice differs from old ratio (e.g., new partner acquires unequally from old partners), premium goes in SACRIFICING ratio. The word 'always' makes this false.
elimination_hints.misconception: Believing goodwill premium always follows old ratio regardless of actual sacrifice.

question_text:
Match the goodwill method on admission with the correct accounting treatment:

Column A (Method):
(i) Premium method — cash retained
(ii) Premium method — cash withdrawn
(iii) Goodwill raised and retained
(iv) Goodwill raised and written off

Column B (Treatment):
(a) Goodwill A/c debited in old ratio, then immediately written off in new ratio; no goodwill appears in Balance Sheet
(b) Cash/Bank debited; old partners' capitals credited in sacrificing ratio; premium stays in business
(c) Goodwill A/c appears as an asset in the new Balance Sheet; old partners' capitals credited in old ratio
(d) Cash/Bank debited; old partners' capitals credited in sacrificing ratio; old partners withdraw the premium
explanation:
(i)→(b): Premium method — cash retained: Cash comes in, credited to old partners in sacrificing ratio, remains in business.
(ii)→(d): Premium method — cash withdrawn: Cash comes in, credited to old partners, then they withdraw it.
(iii)→(c): Goodwill raised and retained: Goodwill A/c is debited and appears in books; old partners credited in old ratio.
(iv)→(a): Goodwill raised and written off: Goodwill raised in old ratio, immediately written off in new ratio, no goodwill in final Balance Sheet.
subtopic: Comprehensive goodwill methods comparison
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Premium cash retained (i) involves Cash/Bank being debited and kept in business, not raising and writing off Goodwill A/c. (i) should map to (b).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing premium method with raise-and-write-off method.

elimination_hints.hint: Goodwill raised and retained (iii) means Goodwill A/c appears in books (c), not Cash/Bank entry (b). Premium method (i) is the one involving Cash/Bank.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the premium method and raised goodwill method.

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses (i) and (ii) — cash retained means premium stays in business (b), cash withdrawn means partners take it out (d).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping retained and withdrawn treatments.

question_text: On admission of a new partner, Sacrificing Ratio = _______ − _______.
explanation: Sacrificing Ratio = Old Ratio − New Ratio (for each old partner). It measures how much each old partner gives up from their old share to accommodate the new partner. If an old partner's sacrificing ratio is negative, that partner has actually gained (gained ratio).
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: Sacrificing ratio formula
subject: accountancy

options.text: Old Ratio; New Ratio
New Ratio; Old Ratio
Old Ratio; Gaining Ratio
New Ratio; Gaining Ratio

elimination_hints.hint: New Ratio − Old Ratio gives the Gaining Ratio, not Sacrificing Ratio. Sacrificing = Old − New (what you had minus what you now have).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the formula — confusing sacrificing with gaining.

elimination_hints.hint: Gaining Ratio is not subtracted from Old Ratio to get Sacrificing Ratio. The formula is simply: Old Ratio − New Ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Introducing gaining ratio into the sacrificing formula unnecessarily.

elimination_hints.hint: This formula has no standard meaning. Sacrificing Ratio = Old Ratio − New Ratio is the standard formula.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up different ratio concepts.

question_text: A, B and C share profits in 4:3:2. D is admitted for 2/9th share. A sacrifices 1/9th from his share and C sacrifices 1/9th from his share. B does not sacrifice. The new profit-sharing ratio is:
explanation:
Old ratios: A = 4/9, B = 3/9, C = 2/9.
A's new share = 4/9 − 1/9 = 3/9.
B's new share = 3/9 (no sacrifice).
C's new share = 2/9 − 1/9 = 1/9.
D's share = 2/9.
New ratio = 3:3:1:2.
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: New ratio when some partners don't sacrifice
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: 4:3:2:2 doesn't account for A and C's sacrifice. Total would be 11 parts, but should be 9. A reduces from 4 to 3, C from 2 to 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Simply adding D's share without reducing sacrificing partners' shares.

elimination_hints.hint: 3:3:2:1 keeps C at 2/9, but C sacrificed 1/9. C's new share should be 2/9 − 1/9 = 1/9.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not reducing C's share despite C's sacrifice.

elimination_hints.hint: 2:3:2:2 has A at 2/9, meaning A sacrificed 2/9. But A only sacrificed 1/9. A's new share = 4/9 − 1/9 = 3/9.
elimination_hints.misconception: Over-calculating A's sacrifice.

question_text: R and S are partners sharing profits in 5:3. T is admitted for 1/4th share. T brings ₹80,000 as premium for goodwill. R and S agree that premium will be shared between them equally. The amount credited to each partner is:
explanation:
Premium = ₹80,000. R and S agree to share premium equally (1:1).
R credited = 80,000 × 1/2 = ₹40,000.
S credited = 80,000 × 1/2 = ₹40,000.
Note: Although old ratio is 5:3, the partners have agreed to share premium equally. When sacrifice is equal, premium is shared equally.
subtopic: Premium shared by agreement (not in old/sacrificing ratio)
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50,000 and ₹30,000 distributes in 5:3 (old ratio). But the question states R and S agreed to share premium EQUALLY, not in old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using old ratio when an explicit equal sharing agreement is given.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹60,000 and ₹20,000 is a 3:1 ratio. The agreed distribution is equal (1:1).
elimination_hints.misconception: Using an incorrect ratio despite clear agreement.

elimination_hints.hint: Both partners agreed to share equally. S must receive ₹40,000 as well.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring S's share in the premium distribution.

question_text:
Scenario: A and B run a furniture business sharing profits in 3:2. They admit C for 1/4th share. Goodwill of the firm is valued at ₹6,00,000. C brings ₹2,00,000 as capital but cannot bring any cash for goodwill. The firm decides to adjust goodwill through capital accounts.

Based on the above scenario, what journal entry will be passed for goodwill adjustment?
explanation:
C's share of goodwill = 1/4 × 6,00,000 = ₹1,50,000. Old partners sacrifice in old ratio 3:2.
Since C cannot bring cash, goodwill is adjusted through capital accounts:
Dr. C's Capital A/c ₹1,50,000
Cr. A's Capital A/c ₹90,000 (1,50,000 × 3/5)
Cr. B's Capital A/c ₹60,000 (1,50,000 × 2/5)
C's effective capital after adjustment = 2,00,000 − 1,50,000 = ₹50,000.
subtopic: Capital adjustment when goodwill not brought in cash
subject: accountancy

options.text: Dr. C's Capital A/c ₹1,50,000; Cr. Goodwill A/c ₹1,50,000
Dr. Goodwill A/c ₹6,00,000; Cr. A's Capital ₹3,60,000; Cr. B's Capital ₹2,40,000
Dr. C's Capital A/c ₹1,50,000; Cr. A's Capital A/c ₹90,000; Cr. B's Capital A/c ₹60,000
Dr. A's Capital A/c ₹90,000; Dr. B's Capital A/c ₹60,000; Cr. C's Capital A/c ₹1,50,000

elimination_hints.hint: No Goodwill A/c is involved in the capital adjustment method. The adjustment is directly between partners' capital accounts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating Goodwill A/c under capital adjustment method.

elimination_hints.hint: This raises full goodwill (₹6,00,000) in books. The question says goodwill is adjusted through capital accounts — only C's share (₹1,50,000) is adjusted, not the full value.
elimination_hints.misconception: Raising full goodwill instead of adjusting only the new partner's share.

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses debit and credit. C PAYS for goodwill (debited), A and B RECEIVE compensation (credited).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing debit and credit entries.

question_text:
Arrange the following steps for determining the new profit-sharing ratio on admission of a partner in the correct order:

(i) Determine each old partner's sacrifice
(ii) Note the old profit-sharing ratio of existing partners
(iii) Calculate the new share of each old partner (Old Share − Sacrifice)
(iv) Determine the new partner's agreed share
(v) State the new profit-sharing ratio of all partners
explanation:
Correct order:
1. (ii) Note the old profit-sharing ratio — this is the starting point.
2. (iv) Determine the new partner's agreed share — this is given in the problem.
3. (i) Determine each old partner's sacrifice — calculate how much each old partner gives up.
4. (iii) Calculate new share of each old partner — Old Share − Sacrifice.
5. (v) State the new ratio — combine all partners' shares.
Order: (ii) → (iv) → (i) → (iii) → (v).
topic: New Profit-Sharing Ratio and Sacrificing Ratio
subtopic: Steps to calculate new profit-sharing ratio
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: You must know the old ratio (ii) BEFORE determining sacrifice (i). Without knowing what each partner had, you can't calculate what they gave up.
elimination_hints.misconception: Starting with the new partner's share before knowing the old shares.

elimination_hints.hint: You can't determine sacrifice (i) before knowing the new partner's share (iv). Sacrifice depends on how much the new partner takes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Trying to calculate sacrifice before knowing the new partner's agreed share.

elimination_hints.hint: Sacrifice (i) cannot be determined first — you need old ratio (ii) and new partner's share (iv) to compute it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attempting to determine sacrifice without prerequisite information.
